question_state: The question asks for the highest daily FRED DCOILBRENTEU (EIA Brent spot price) observation across May 14–15, 2026. The metric is an EIA-assessed daily spot price, not an ICE futures settlement, which introduces a potential gap vs most news-reported prices. As of May 14, the spot price was ~$105.50–$105.76; the May 15 observation was not yet available. The forecast relied heavily on Kalshi prediction market implied prices and geopolitical risk assessment to project the May 15 value.
factors_tracked: Current Brent spot price level (May 14 anchor)
Prediction market implied prices (Kalshi, Manifold, Robinhood)
Data source inconsistency (futures settlement vs spot price)

evidence_surfaced.claim: May 14 intraday Brent at $105.42–$105.76/barrel across CNBC, NDTV, Economic Times — anchors the May 14 observation well below the predicted max
Kalshi prediction market for May 15 Brent implies median ~$107, with P(>$106.99)=49%, P(>$108.99)=23%, P(>$110.99)=10%
EIA STEO forecasts Brent averaging $106/bbl for May–June 2026
U.S. rejected Iran peace proposal May 13; Brent July futures settled at $107.77 (up $3.56)
Brent July settled at $105.63 on May 13 (down $2.14) — directly contradicts Sprague's $107.77 figure for the same date
China crude imports fell 20% YoY in April; demand destruction could cap upside

judgment_trajectory: Initial scan: searched for FRED DCOILBRENTEU current value and general oil price forecasts — found broad $95–115 analyst range
Located May 14 intraday prices ~$105.50, establishing a floor well below any likely maximum
Discovered Kalshi prediction market implying ~$107 median for May 15 — became primary quantitative anchor
Found conflicting May 13 settlement data ($107.77 vs $105.63) — flagged as unresolved but leaned toward Sprague's more detailed report
Incorporated EIA $106/bbl average forecast as consistency check
Final synthesis: max of May 14 (~$105.75) and Kalshi-implied May 15 (~$107.50) = $107.50
what_worked: Kalshi prediction market provided a direct, quantitative implied distribution for May 15 Brent — the single most useful evidence source
Triangulating May 14 intraday prices across three independent sources (CNBC, NDTV, Economic Times) gave a solid anchor
EIA STEO $106/bbl average forecast served as a useful sanity check on the prediction market median
Searching for prediction markets (Kalshi, Manifold, Robinhood) rather than just news articles yielded forward-looking probabilistic information
what_misled: Conflicting May 13 settlement prices ($107.77 vs $105.63) consumed significant verification effort without resolution — likely reflects spot vs futures confusion
Multiple searches returned the same Business Standard article repeatedly, creating search redundancy
The distinction between ICE futures settlement prices and EIA spot price assessments was not clarified until late in the run, potentially biasing the calibration upward
unresolved_concerns: FRED DCOILBRENTEU is EIA spot price assessment, which may differ materially from ICE futures settlement prices cited in most news reports — the $107.77 Sprague figure is a futures settlement, not spot
Conflicting May 13 data ($107.77 vs $105.63) was never fully resolved; if the lower figure is correct, the geopolitical risk premium may be overstated
Trump-Xi summit outcome on May 15 could cause a sharp move in either direction — a surprise Iran breakthrough could drop prices $5–10
FRED data released with 1-day lag; May 15 observation may not appear until May 16, creating verification uncertainty
Manifold market's 54% probability of Brent ≥ $110 suggests a non-trivial tail risk of a higher outcome, though liquidity is low
